title: Wave packet landscape in linear open quantum systems
authors: Kang Xu, Miao-Miao Yi, Zi-Hong Yan, C. P. Sun
abstract: We develop a quantum landscape approach to characterize the long-time behavior of wave packet spreading in linear open quantum systems. Instead of treating diffusion, localization, and collapse of the wave packet as separate dynamical phenomena, we show that they originate from the symmetry structure of an underlying landscape in covariance space. The geometry of this landscape determines these distinct long time behaviors. Trapping potentials and bath fluctuations act as distinct symmetry-breaking perturbations, leading to noncommuting long-time limits and abrupt changes in the asymptotic wave-packet width. This geometric picture provides a unified origin for wave-packet diffusion, localization, and collapse in dissipative quantum dynamics.
summary: This paper introduces a quantum landscape approach to analyze how wave packets evolve in open quantum systems. It shows that the long-time behavior—whether the packet collapses, localizes, or diffuses—is determined by the underlying geometry of a potential landscape in the covariance space. This geometric picture unifies these distinct dissipative quantum dynamics.
main_problem: To develop a quantum landscape approach to characterize the long-time behavior of wave packet spreading in linear open quantum systems.
main_result: The long-time dynamics (diffusion, localization, collapse) are unified by the geometry of a potential landscape in covariance space, showing that symmetry breaking dictates the asymptotic behavior.
method:
The dynamics of the covariance matrix are mapped onto a generalized gradient flow equation, $\dot{\sigma} = -M
abla L(\sigma)$, where $L$ is the quantum landscape function.
model_or_system: The system consists of linear open quantum systems, modeled by coupled harmonic oscillators interacting with a bath (Caldeira-Leggett type). The dynamics are governed by the quantum Langevin equation.
key_observables: The covariance matrix $\Sigma$ (or its vectorization $\sigma$), and the position width $\Delta q(t)$.
important_parameters: The oscillator frequency ($\omega$), damping rate ($\Gamma$), and the presence/strength of bath fluctuations ($\xi(t)$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on casting the covariance evolution into a gradient flow form and analyzing the zero modes of the system matrix.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the deformation of the landscape: a bowl shape for confined systems (implying collapse) versus a flat valley for free particles, which becomes tilted by fluctuations.
paper_structure: The paper first introduces the landscape approach to the covariance dynamics, then analyzes specific cases (e.g., harmonic oscillator vs. free particle) to demonstrate how the landscape geometry dictates the asymptotic state, culminating in a discussion of symmetry breaking due to fluctuations.
why_interesting: This work provides a powerful geometric framework to unify disparate phenomena (diffusion, localization, collapse) in open quantum systems, offering deeper physical insight than standard master equation treatments.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Landscape deformation by trap potential in fluctuation-free dynamics. The landscapes are shown in the (∆q, ∆p) projection, with the remaining covariance fixed at its minimizing value. The axes are extended to negative values of ∆q and ∆p only for visualizing the landscape geometry, although physical variances are restricted to be nonnegative. (a) For ω ̸= 0, the fluctuation-free landscape is bowl-shaped and has a unique minimum, implying collapse of the wave packet in the long-time limit. Here the parameters are set as ω = γ = 1. (b) For ω = 0, the fluctuation-free landscape develops a flat valley of degenerate minima, reflecting translational symmetry in covariance space and...

FIG. 2: Landscape deformation by fluctuation. The landscapes are shown in the (∆q, ∆p) projection, with the remaining covariance fixed at its minimizing value. The translucent surfaces denote the fluctuation-free landscapes, whereas the solid surfaces denote the landscapes deformed by bath fluctuations. The parameters are set as γ = 1, ∆qξ = ∆pξ = 0.5. (a) In the confined case, the fluctuation preserves the bowl topology but shifts its minimum away from the origin, leading to a stationary wave packet with finite width. (b) In the free-particle case, the fluctuation adds a linear term along the flat direction, tilts the fluctuation-free valley, removes the degenerate minimum manifold, and...
